name: remote
context: Manage remote repositories — add, fetch, pull, push, and configure origin and upstream remotes
info: Remotes are named references to other git repositories, typically hosted on GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket. The default remote is 'origin' (where you cloned from). This skill covers adding and removing remotes, fetching to download remote state without merging, pulling to fetch-and-merge in one step, pushing to upload local commits, and managing upstream tracking branches. Agents must understand the fetch-before-push pattern: always fetch to check for remote changes before pushing. Force-pushing (git push --force) is dangerous and should only be used on personal branches after rebase. See [[git/branch]] for tracking branch relationships, [[git/merge]] for integrating fetched changes, and [[git/config]] for remote URL and credential configuration.
body:
# Git Remote

Remotes connect your local repository to copies hosted elsewhere. Most workflows involve one remote (origin), but contributing to open source typically uses two (origin for your fork, upstream for the original).

## Cloning

```bash
# Clone via HTTPS
git clone https://github.com/owner/repo.git

# Clone via SSH
git clone git@github.com:owner/repo.git

# Clone into a specific directory
git clone https://github.com/owner/repo.git my-project

# Shallow clone (last N commits only — faster for large repos)
git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/owner/repo.git

# Clone a specific branch
git clone -b develop https://github.com/owner/repo.git
```

Cloning automatically sets up the `origin` remote pointing to the clone URL.

## Managing Remotes

```bash
# List remotes
git remote -v

# Add a remote
git remote add upstream https://github.com/original/repo.git

# Rename a remote
git remote rename origin old-origin

# Remove a remote
git remote remove upstream

# Change remote URL
git remote set-url origin git@github.com:owner/repo.git

# Show detailed remote info
git remote show origin
```

## Fetching

Fetch downloads objects and refs from a remote without modifying your working tree:

```bash
# Fetch from origin
git fetch origin

# Fetch from all remotes
git fetch --all

# Fetch and prune stale remote-tracking branches
git fetch --prune

# Fetch a specific branch
git fetch origin main

# Fetch tags
git fetch --tags
```

After fetching, remote-tracking branches (e.g., `origin/main`) are updated. Use [[git/merge]] or [[git/rebase]] to integrate.

## Pulling

Pull = fetch + merge (or fetch + rebase with `--rebase`):

```bash
# Pull current branch from tracking remote
git pull

# Pull with rebase instead of merge
git pull --rebase

# Pull a specific branch
git pull origin main

# Pull and auto-stash dirty working tree
git pull --autostash
```

**Prefer `git pull --rebase`** for feature branches to maintain linear history. Configure as default:
```bash
git config --global pull.rebase true
```

## Pushing

```bash
# Push current branch to tracking remote
git push

# Push and set upstream tracking
git push -u origin feature-branch

# Push a specific branch
git push origin main

# Push all branches
git push --all

# Push tags
git push --tags
git push origin v1.0.0  # specific tag

# Delete a remote branch
git push origin --delete feature-done
```

### Force Push

```bash
# Force push (DANGEROUS — overwrites remote history)
git push --force

# Safer: force-with-lease (fails if remote has new commits you haven't fetched)
git push --force-with-lease
```

**Always prefer `--force-with-lease` over `--force`.** It prevents overwriting commits others have pushed. Only force-push personal feature branches after rebase — never force-push main or shared branches.

## Fork and Upstream Workflow

Common for open-source contributions:

```bash
# Clone your fork
git clone git@github.com:you/repo.git

# Add upstream (original repo)
git remote add upstream https://github.com/original/repo.git

# Sync your fork with upstream
git fetch upstream
git checkout main
git merge upstream/main
git push origin main

# Alternative: rebase onto upstream
git fetch upstream
git rebase upstream/main
git push origin main
```

## Agent Best Practices

1. **Always fetch before push** — `git fetch origin && git push` avoids surprises
2. **Use --force-with-lease** — never bare `--force` unless you've verified nobody else pushed
3. **Use -u on first push** — `git push -u origin branch` sets tracking for future push/pull
4. **Prune regularly** — `git fetch --prune` removes stale remote-tracking branches
5. **Use SSH for automation** — SSH keys don't expire like tokens and support key-based auth
6. **Shallow clone for CI** — `git clone --depth 1` is much faster for build pipelines

sections.title: Cloning repositories
sections.body: `git clone <url>` creates a local copy with `origin` set to the clone URL. Clone via HTTPS (requires token/password) or SSH (requires key). Use `--depth N` for shallow clones (faster, less disk). Use `-b <branch>` to check out a non-default branch. Use `--recurse-submodules` for repos with submodules.

sections.title: Fetching remote changes
sections.body: `git fetch <remote>` downloads new objects and updates remote-tracking branches (e.g., `origin/main`) without touching your working tree or local branches. This is always safe. Use `--all` to fetch all remotes, `--prune` to clean up deleted remote branches, `--tags` to fetch tags. After fetching, integrate with [[git/merge]] or [[git/rebase]].

sections.title: Pulling (fetch + merge/rebase)
sections.body: `git pull` fetches and merges the tracking branch. `git pull --rebase` fetches and rebases instead (preferred for feature branches). `git pull --autostash` stashes dirty working tree, pulls, then re-applies the stash. Configure `pull.rebase true` globally to make rebase the default. See [[git/merge]] and [[git/rebase]] for the underlying operations.

sections.title: Pushing local commits
sections.body: `git push` uploads local commits to the remote. First push: `git push -u origin <branch>` sets tracking. `git push --tags` pushes all tags. `git push origin --delete <branch>` deletes a remote branch. **Force push**: `--force-with-lease` is safe (checks for remote changes); `--force` is dangerous (overwrites unconditionally). Never force-push shared branches.

sections.title: Adding and configuring remotes
sections.body: `git remote add <name> <url>` adds a new remote. `git remote set-url <name> <url>` changes the URL. `git remote -v` lists all remotes with URLs. `git remote show <name>` shows detailed info including tracking branches. Common setup: `origin` for your fork/copy, `upstream` for the original repository.

links.context: Merge fetched changes into local branches
Commits are what you push and pull
GitHub is the most common remote host

related.note: Tracking branches connect local branches to remote counterparts
Pull performs a merge by default after fetching
Pull --rebase performs a rebase after fetching
GitHub repos are the most common remote targets

scripts.name: Remote Sync
scripts.description: Fetch, check for divergence, and push or pull as needed
scripts.inline:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

REMOTE="${1:-origin}"
BRANCH=$(git branch --show-current)

echo "Syncing $BRANCH with $REMOTE/$BRANCH"

# Fetch latest
git fetch "$REMOTE"

# Check if remote branch exists
if ! git rev-parse --verify "$REMOTE/$BRANCH" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "Remote branch $REMOTE/$BRANCH does not exist."
  echo "Push with: git push -u $REMOTE $BRANCH"
  exit 0
fi

# Count ahead/behind
LOCAL=$(git rev-parse HEAD)
REMOTE_HEAD=$(git rev-parse "$REMOTE/$BRANCH")
BASE=$(git merge-base HEAD "$REMOTE/$BRANCH")

if [ "$LOCAL" = "$REMOTE_HEAD" ]; then
  echo "Already up to date."
elif [ "$LOCAL" = "$BASE" ]; then
  BEHIND=$(git rev-list --count HEAD.."$REMOTE/$BRANCH")
  echo "Behind by $BEHIND commit(s). Pulling..."
  git pull --rebase "$REMOTE" "$BRANCH"
elif [ "$REMOTE_HEAD" = "$BASE" ]; then
  AHEAD=$(git rev-list --count "$REMOTE/$BRANCH"..HEAD)
  echo "Ahead by $AHEAD commit(s). Pushing..."
  git push "$REMOTE" "$BRANCH"
else
  AHEAD=$(git rev-list --count "$REMOTE/$BRANCH"..HEAD)
  BEHIND=$(git rev-list --count HEAD.."$REMOTE/$BRANCH")
  echo "Diverged: $AHEAD ahead, $BEHIND behind."
  echo "Recommend: git pull --rebase $REMOTE $BRANCH && git push"
fi

depends.reason: Remote URLs and credentials must be configured

author: dojo-community
